coredata.dc:title: Efficient microwave induction to modify surface for high-rate lithium/fluorinated carbon battery with ultra-high power density
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Gaobang
Fan, Yong
Yu, Tianhui
Shoaib, Muhammad
Khan, Azim
Liu, Yifan
Jian, Xian
coredata.dc:description: The practical application of high-rate lithium/fluorinated carbon (Li/CFx) primary batteries faces challenge of relatively slow electrode reaction kinetics limited by inert C-F bond and strong polarity. Herein, an effective microwave-induction strategy was developed to regulate C-F bond for 80C high-rate Li/CFx battery with suppressed polarization. The as-mentioned microwave method was carried out in the alcohol-based solvent for uniformly modify CFx, including precise defluorination, C-F bond regulation, surface functional groups and carbon quantum dots. Precise defluorination contains the conversion of C-F bond to semi-ionic C-F bond while preserving C-F2 and C-F3. During the process of C-F bond regulation, hydroxyl groups is grafted onto the surface of CFx to increase the wettability and electronegativity of the electrode. Compared with raw CFx, the Li/CFx batteries using microwave-modified CFx achieved high discharge rate of 80C possessing a high power density of 134.091 kW/kg. In addition, the optimal Li/CFx battery possesses a large specific capacity increase ratio of 142.6 % at 50C. This microwave-induction approach for modifying CFx in ethanol-based solvent provides a facile and effective pathway to design high-rate Li/CFx battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave induction
Surface modification
Ultra-high power density
Li/CFx battery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of microwave-induction technology for CFx including magnetic stirring, microwave modification and centrifugation processes. (b) The mechanism of microwave induced vibration and friction of CFx and in-situ grafting of functional groups such as –OH groups, and the application of modified CFx to cathode of primary batteries.
The micromorphology observation of (a-c) raw CFx and (d-f) CFx-80 by FESEM. EDS mapping results of (g) raw CFx and (h) CFx-80.

(a) XRD patterns of raw CFx and microwave modified materials including CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 at as-designed power density of 30 %, 50 % and 80 %, respectively. FT-IR spectra of (b) raw CFx and (c) CFx-80. (d) Raman spectra of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80. (e-h) High-resolution XPS spectra on C 1 s and F 1 s states of raw CFx and CFx-80. (i) TG curves of raw CFx and CFx-80.
HRTEM images of (a-c) raw CFx and (d-f) CFx-80. (g) Local carbon layer, (h) carbon quantum dots and (i) curled carbon structure on the surface of CFx-80.

Galvanostatic discharge curves of Li/CFx batteries based on (a) raw CFx, (b) CFx-30, (c) CFx-50 and (d) CFx-80 cathodes at different discharge rate. (e) EIS Nyquist plots of various cathodes before the discharge process. (f) GITT curves of various cathodes. Comparison on (g) specific capacity and (h) specific energy of various cathodes.

(a) Summary of the discharge rate, power density and energy density of Li/CFx batteries reported by literatures. (b) The DOS of the raw CFx and modified CFx (White, black, green and red spheres represent hydrogen, carbon, fluorine and oxygen atoms, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Efficient microwave induction to modify surface for high-rate lithium/fluorinated carbon battery with ultra-high power density

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The practical application of high-rate lithium/fluorinated carbon (Li/CFx) primary batteries faces challenge of relatively slow electrode reaction kinetics limited by inert C-F bond and strong polarity. Herein, an effective microwave-induction strategy was developed to regulate C-F bond for 80C high-rate Li/CFx battery with suppressed polarization. The as-mentioned microwave method was carried out in the alcohol-based solvent for uniformly modify CFx, including precise defluorination, C-F bond regulation, surface functional groups and carbon quantum dots. Precise defluorination contains the conversion of C-F bond to semi-ionic C-F bond while preserving C-F2 and C-F3. During the process of C-F bond regulation, hydroxyl groups is grafted onto the surface of CFx to increase the wettability and electronegativity of the electrode. Compared with raw CFx, the Li/CFx batteries using microwave-modified CFx achieved high discharge rate of 80C possessing a high power density of 134.091 kW/kg. In addition, the optimal Li/CFx battery possesses a large specific capacity increase ratio of 142.6 % at 50C. This microwave-induction approach for modifying CFx in ethanol-based solvent provides a facile and effective pathway to design high-rate Li/CFx battery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The remarkable characteristics of lithium primary batteries (LPBs) have drawn attention because of their wide operating temperatures, long service life and high energy density [1–3]. LPBs are commonly used in industrial equipment, measurement tools, medical devices and military applications [4–6]. Among the various types of LPBs, the combination of fluorinated carbon cathode and metal lithium anode (Li/CFx) stands out due to its exceptional electrochemical coupling with a high theoretical capacity of 865 mAh/g and a high theoretical energy density of 2180 Wh/kg [7–9]. Lithium metal ion batteries have also received widespread attention from researchers due to their ultra-high energy density. For example, Chen group developed a novel friction-induced organic/inorganic hybrid interface enabling ≤ 20 μm thin free-standing lithium strips for lithium metal batteries, which provides a scalable approach concerning tribology design for producing practical thin free-standing lithium metal anodes and high-performance thin Li metal anodes [10–12]. CFx is a typical non-stoichiometric compound, characterized by a range of x between 0 and 2. The specific value of x, representing the fluorine to carbon ratio, is influenced by both the synthesis process and the structural properties of precursor carbon material [13–17].

However, CFx demonstrates a relatively low electrical conductivity, which is responsible for the presence of covalent C-F bonds [18–20]. The performance of Li/CFx batteries are hindered by insulated nature of CFx and intrinsic polarization, primarily ascribed to the sp3 hybridization of covalent C-F bonds [21]. This effect is particularly pronounced in CFx with a high fluorine to carbon ratio. The drawbacks associated with this insulation include limited rate capability, significant polarization upon discharge and the occurrence of excessive heat generation during battery operation [22]. Increasing the conductivity of CFx electrode is an effective pathway to improve rate performance of battery [23,24]. In order to solve these limitations of relatively low electrical conductivity, the methods used by researchers mainly include material doping [25,26], surface modification [27–29], preparation of composite electrodes [30]. Delun Zhu et al. prepared sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CFx using a one-step heteroatom doping method and proved that it produced conductive interfaces and excellent charge conduction ability to improve electrochemical performance [31].

In addition to the commonly used modification of CFx, some investigations cover on special equipment to modify CFx to boost electrochemical properties of Li/CFx batteries such as plasma [32–34], ultraviolet–radiation (UV-radiation) [35]. Plasma modified approach for CFx is characterized by high equipment cost, complex operation process and long processing time. Meanwhile, it attains uncertain damage to the surface of material, and the modified product is not uniform. Therefore, the practical application of plasma modification technology for CFx is difficult and needs improvement. The energy of UV–radiation is small then corresponding UV-method needs a long time of more than 24 h. The modification of CFx by UV-radiation mainly occurred in CFx surface, and modification depth was relatively shallow which was not sufficient for applications requiring deep modification. Compared with plasma and UV-radiation methods, microwave-induction approach for CFx possesses the advantages of high efficiency and short time consuming, energy saving, environmental protection, good modification effect and simple operation [36–38]. Microwave heating principle is making use of the interaction between microwave and material molecules to generate heat energy through vibration and friction of molecules [39,40]. In microwave heating process, the positive and negative charges of polar molecules are not evenly distributed, and they are more sensitive to electromagnetic fields. Microwave is a low-cost modification method with broad application prospects and widely used.

In this paper, an alcohol-based solvent liquid phase microwave-induction strategy was developed to modify CFx for Li/CFx battery. Appling microwave-induction thermal effect to graft hydroxyl (–OH) groups in alcohol-based solvent, –OH groups and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in situ grew on CFx surface which was effectively reduce resistance of CFx and increase diffusion coefficient of lithium ion. Compared with raw CFx, microwave-modified CFx achieves a high power density as well as 80C high discharge rate.

An alcohol-based liquid phase microwave-induction strategy was used to synthesize modified CFx by using a mixed solution of ethylene glycol (AR, 98.5 %, Aladdin Co., Ltd.) and alcohol (AR, 95 %, Aladdin Co., Ltd.) with a volume ratio of 1:1 in beakers mixing with CFx. The commercial CFx was acquired from Xiamen Ceffone New Energy Technology Co., Ltd (product number 103095). Then, the CFx in this solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min. The mixed solution was treated by microcomputer microwave chemical reactor at 30 %, 50 % and 80 % power for 360 s (the reactor has five inherent powers of 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 80 % and 100 %, corresponding to a power of 800 W for 100 %). The treated solution was centrifuged and freeze-dried for 48 h, then stored at 60 ℃ in a drying oven. The microwave-modified CFx corresponding to the assembled coin batteries were named as CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80.

The microscopic morphology and elements distribution of CFx were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL2100F) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Sigma300). The phase and structure of CFx were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV) with an angle range of 10° to 90°. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) is used to confirm the functional groups of CFx. Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution) was recorded to analyze the molecular structures of CFx using a laser with the wavelength of 532 nm in a Raman shift range of 1000–2000 cm−1. The chemical valence state and element type of CFx were detected with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was applied to perform the stability of CFx.

The CR2032 coin batteries were utilized for conducting the electrochemical tests. The cathode consisted of an 8:1:1 (wt%) mixture of CFx, super P and polyvinylidene fluoride dispersing in N-methylpyrrolidone to form a slurry coating on Al foil. Then, the dried slurry is cut into small pieces and squeezed to get cathode. The cathode materials were assembled into coin batteries and discharged with a cut-off potential of 1.5 V. Galvanostatic intermittent titration (GITT) and constant-current discharge were designed at 25 °C to confirm the electrochemical performance including specific capacity, potential, energy density and diffusion coefficients of lithium ions using a device of Xinwei company of China (MH W-20060CH). The as-prepared CFx cathode was assembled in CR2032 batteries to test electrochemical performance. An electrochemical workstation (CHI760E) was applied to recorded the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a frequency range of 0.01 ∼ 100000 Hz.

The CASTEP software package was applied to investigate the bandgap and electron density of the raw CFx and the CFx with –OH groups using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The DFT calculations were conducted within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) using Perdew-Burke Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation functional. Raw CFx was modelled as a single layer containing 32 fluorine atoms and 32 carbon atoms. A cut-off energy of 340 eV was chosen for the plane-wave expansion. The displacement, convergence tolerances of energy and maximum force were 0.015 Å, 1 × 10−5 eV/atom and 0.03 eV/Å, respectively.

Traditional solid-phase modification techniques often face challenge of material non-uniformity. Herein, we proposed a facile and effective approach to modify CFx in liquid-phase to get high-rate CFx as shown in Fig. 1 . In detail, as-modified CFx with surface functional groups was firstly synthesized in an alcohol-based liquid with microwave-induction strategy, thereafter, adopting centrifugation and freeze-drying processes as schematically depicted in Fig. 1 (a). After optical observation, modified CFx presents a dark yellow colour compared with the raw CFx of light yellow, which confirms that microwave has an effect on CFx. In theory, microwave denotes electromagnetic wave that possesses frequencies ranging from 300 MHz to 300 GHz. Polar molecules are heated by friction behaviour due to repeated rapid orientation rotation in the microwave high-frequency electric field. The mechanism diagram of applying ethanol and ethylene glycol as solvent to modify CFx is shown in Fig. 1 (b). Both ethanol and ethylene glycol are polar molecules containing –OH groups, resulting in modified CFx grafted by –OH groups after microwave treatment. The modified CFx is utilized in high-rate Li/CFx batteries.

The microstructure and surface morphology of raw CFx and CFx-80 (the power source is controlled at 80 %) were observed by FESEM. Fig. 2
(a-c) and Fig. 2 (d-f) show the typical and micron scale block structure of raw CFx and CFx-80, respectively. Compared with raw CFx as shown in Fig. 2 a. CFx-80 shown in Fig. 2 d maintains the bulk morphology of raw CFx. There is a large surface roughness on the surfaces of raw CFx and CFx-80, which increases the specific surface area of CFx. Interestingly, compared with raw CFx in Fig. 2 (c), a large amount of cracks appeared on the surface of CFx-80 after microwave modification as shown in Fig. 2 (f). The increase of the specific surface area of CFx is favourable to raise the electrode surface area in contact with electrolyte and the electrode’s electrochemical activity, and thus improve the discharge capacity. Meanwhile, it is beneficial to maintain a high potential platform and improve Li/CFx batteries with high-rate performance.

The corresponding energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of raw CFx and CFx-80 are recorded as shown in Fig. 2 (g-h). The element distribution of F and C of CFx is shown in Fig. 2 (g). After microwave modification, CFx-80 remains a uniform element distribution of C and F shown in Fig. 2 (h) indicating that the distribution of elements is consistency. In Fig. S1, the contents of F and C elements of CFx-80 and raw CFx maintain at the same level, the content of O increased from 2.26 to 2.74 wt%, and the content of F decreased from 37.23 to 32.56 wt%. The results suggested that the alcohol-based liquid phase played a role in the modification of CFx and achieved defluorination.

To further understand the structure and surface properties of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80, the test of XRD, FT-IR, Raman and high-resolution XPS were carried out and analysed. The XRD patterns of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are exhibited in Fig. 3
(a). The 2θ about 13.1° and 41.3° are assigned to C-F (001) and C-C in-plane (100), respectively [31]. In XRD pattern of CFx, high fluorine compounds are represented by the C-F (001), whereas the in-plane graphite network is represented by the C-C (100). Compared with raw CFx, the consistency of the C-C (100) strengths of CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 is due to the preservation of original structure of CFx after microwave modification.

The chemical structural properties of CFx were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. Both raw CFx and CFx-80 possess the obvious absorption peaks at 3450 and 3430 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 3 (b), which correspond to the stretching vibration characteristic of the O-H bond, respectively. And the bands of raw CFx and CFx-80 at 1637 cm−1 are assigned to C=C. The formation of hydrogen bond changes the vibration mode of –OH groups, resulting in the change of the position of its infrared absorption peak to generate a redshift. After microwave-induction process, more hydrogen bonds are formed among –OH groups and other polar groups. The interaction between –OH groups and C-F bonds in CFx lead to a change in the chemical bonding state. The redshift reduces band gap and increase electrochemical activity of CFx. The two shoulder bands located near 1088 and 1328 cm−1 are assigned to the semi-ionic C-F and perfluorinated C-F2/C-F3 bonds as shown in Fig. 3 (c), respectively [41].

The Raman spectrum of raw CFx, and microwave modified samples of CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are shown in Fig. 3 (d). Four samples all display two distinctive Raman peaks identified as D and G bands. The D band is the defect-induced peak, and the G band is attached to the sp2 hybridized carbon [42]. The level of structural disorder in the samples is assessed by the integral area ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG). The corresponding ID/IG values of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are 0.31, 0.61, 0.76 and 0.94, respectively. The value of ID/IG and their peak intensity show an increasing trend, illustrating that more carbon is exposed after microwave modification, which is consistent with the results of relative element content observed by FESEM.

High-resolution XPS was utilized to conduct a more in-depth analysis for the bonding nature of elements and chemical components. As shown in Fig. 3 (e), detailed chemical states of raw CFx are observed through high-resolution spectra on the C 1 s state, which can be deconvoluted into four subpeaks showing an increase in binding energy: C=C/C-C (284.8 eV), semi-ionic C-F (287.4 eV), covalent C-F (290.1 eV) and perfluorinated C-F2/C-F3 (292.0 eV) species. The similar four subpeaks with increasing binding energy of CFx-80 is given in Fig. 3 (g). The deconvolution of F 1 s spectra of raw CFx reveals the presence of three prominent subpeaks in Fig. 3 (f). As shown in Fig. 3 (h), raw CFx is consistent with CFx-80. After deconvolution of C 1 s of raw CFx and CFx-80, the corresponding atomic percentages are summarized in Table S1. The atomic percentage of C=C/C-C is increased from 4.54 to 5.94 %, and semi-ionic C-F is increased from 18.37 to 18.62 %, which are conducive to improving conductivity of CFx and thus improving rate performance of the battery. As shown in Fig. S2, the O 1 s spectra is fitted to four subpeaks with binding energies of 529.0, 531.6, 532.7 and 535.1 eV which are assigned to lattice oxygen (Olat), oxygen vacancy (Ovac), hydroxylated oxygen (O-OH), and surface adsorbed H2O and O2 (Oads), respectively [41]. The content of hydroxyl oxygen increased from 26.03 to 42.33 % before and after microwave treatment of CFx. TG curves of raw CFx and CFx-80 are shown in Fig. 3 (i), the remaining mass of CFx-80 is increasing, indicating a reduction in the fluorine content and fluorine to carbon ratio of CFx due to defluorination after microwave modification. Defluorination and C-F regulation is beneficial to improve electrical conductivity of CFx.

The HRTEM is a test and characterization method to observe the crystalline structure and crystal morphology of CFx. The HRTEM images of raw CFx are shown in Fig. 4
(a-c), from which the raw CFx is found to be a typical amorphous material [43]. Interestingly, after microwave modification, the surface of CFx-80 generates CQDs shown in Fig. 4 (d-f), which is consistent with the existing microwave preparation of CQDs [44]. In turn of reaction mechanism, alcohols are first converted into aldehydes. Aldehydes react with alcohols to form unsaturated aldehydes, which further condense and polymerize to form long carbon chains. Long carbon chains crosslink and dehydrate to form CQDs with carbon nuclei and surface functional groups. In Fig. 4 (f), CQDs appears on the surface of CFx-80 shown a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm which is agree with the report by Wang et al
[45]. The CQDs generated on the surface of CFx-80 are consistent with the increase in the atomic percentage of C=C/C-C in high-resolution XPS C 1 s. The EDS mappings of raw CFx and CFx-80 were observed by HRTEM in Fig. S3 (a-b). After microwave modification, the distribution of C, F and O on the surface of CFx-80 is uniform indicating that the integrity of the CFx-80 structure maintains the bulk morphology which is consistent with raw CFx. From the color contrast of the distribution of O, it is seen that the O resolution of CFx-80 is more pronounced than the raw CFx. Interestingly, microwave modification of CFx in ethanol-based solvents resulted in carbon modification on the surface of CFx including local carbon layer, a large amount of CQDs and curved carbon structures. A nanoscale local carbon layer was formed on the surface of the CFx, as shown by the white dashed line in Fig. 4 (g). This ultrafine conducting layer formation improve the CFx stability and prevent the capacity degradation of Li/CFx batteries. Meanwhile, as shown by the green dashed line in Fig. 4 (h), a large number of CQDs generated on the surface of the modified material. In addition, curled carbon structures were also observed on the surface of fluorinated carbon, as shown by the brown dashed line in Fig. 4 (i). There is a significant difference between the carbon structure in the lower left corner and the crystal structure in the upper right corner. The modified carbon on the surface of CFx is conducive to increase electrical conductivity and improving electrochemical performance of CFx.

In order to ascertain the performance of microwave-modified CFx cathode, galvanostatic discharge behaviours of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 as Li/CFx battery cathodes were assessed under different discharge rates adhering to a fixed cut-off potential of 1.5 V. Based on the discharge curves depicted in Fig. 5
(a), the raw CFx delivers a discharge specific capacity of 773.04mAh/g at 0.01C. However, discharge specific capacity and potential show a tendency for rapid fade, coupled with an unstable potential platform and the occurrence of potential hysteresis phenomena. The poor rate capability and power density of the raw CFx cathode are evidently due to its slow reaction kinetics resulting from insufficient intrinsic electronic conductivity and the build-up of inactive LiF product on its surface. Especially, the decrease in the potential platform alongside the discharge rate is associated with an increased polarization. The polarization of the surface of CFx is usually closely related to molecular structure, chemical composition, surface charge distribution and environment, etc. The introduce of –OH groups on CFx favor the reaction between CFx and metallic Li, then reducing polarization, increasing their wettability with the electrolyte and improving their electrochemical performance.

In contrast, the CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 cathodes show an enhanced rate capability, especially the latter as shown in Fig. 5 (b-d). Generally, below 10C, the specific capacity of three electrodes is similar to raw CFx, and the potential platform is stable. The potential platform of four electrodes is about 3.1 V at 0.01C. The rate performance of raw CFx is poor with a specific capacity of 198.08 mAh/g at 50C. Interestingly, the CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 cathodes in lithium primary batteries are capable of discharging up to 80C. Discharge rate above 20C, the energy density and specific capacity of CFx-80 are better than other electrodes, especially the discharge rate is 50C as shown in Table S2 and Table S3. At a discharge rate of 50C, the specific capacities of raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 electrodes are 198.08, 262.57, 493.44 and 506.76 mAh/g, respectively. The CFx-80 battery possesses a capacity increase ratio of 142.6 % compared with raw CFx at 50C. From the plot of 50C, it is seen that with the increase of microwave power, the discharge platform of Li/CFx batteries is more obvious and stable. The experiment employing even higher microwave powers was added with the maximum power up to 100 % as shown in Fig. S4. The microwave-modified CFx shows a good rate performance, indicating that the organic liquid phase microwave-induction strategy is a feasible way to boost rate performance of CFx.

In order to assess the charge transfer characteristics more comprehensively, Nyquist plots obtained from EIS were tested from Li/CFx batteries as shown in Fig. 5 (e). All spectra consist of semicircles in the high frequency region which are assigned to charge-transfer reactions occurring at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte (Rct), followed by a linear Warburg tail in the low frequency region which is indicative of the resistance to ion diffusion (Rw) [46]. Obviously, the Rct of Li/CFx batteries based on CFx-80 (67.84 Ω) is much lower than those based on raw CFx (89.28 Ω), CFx-30 (80.58 Ω) and CFx-50 (76.25 Ω) shown in Fig. S5. It indicates that CFx-80 electrode possesses a much faster reaction kinetics and conductivity advantages, agreeing with energy density and rate capability.

The measurement of lithium ion diffusion coefficients (DLi +) in various cathodes is carried out using the GITT for obtaining a thorough comprehension of the discharge process kinetics as shown Fig. 5 (f). The batteries with raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 electrodes were discharged at 0.1C with a 20 min interval followed by a 240 min open circuit stand, to ensure the battery potential were relaxed to steady-state value (Es ). According to the single step of GITT for raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 cathodes in Fig. S6, the potential of lithium primary battery changes from initial potential E0 to relaxation potential ES . The potential change value caused by relaxation process is ΔES . The potential change caused by pulse process is ΔEτ after discharging for 20 min of Li/CFx battery. The DLi + was determined by analysing the slope within the linear range based on Fick’s second law with a comprehensive explanation available in the Supporting Information [47]. In the potential range of 1.5 ∼ 3.0 V, the DLi + of Li/CFx batteries with raw CFx, CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are all in the range of 10-15 ∼ 10-12 cm2·s shown in Fig. S7. With a decrease of discharge potential, DLi + increases gradually indicating that the ion conductivity of Li/CFx battery increases gradually. This is because the chemical reaction that occurs in discharge process of Li/CFx battery to produce conductive carbon. The conductivity of conductive carbon is better than CFx and the carbon reduces the energy barrier of electron diffusion.

In order to better understand the effect of microwave-modified CFx on batteries performance, the specific capacity and energy of batteries at various rates were statistically summarized in Fig. 5 (g-h) and the corresponding data are shown in Table S2 and Table S3. Compared with CFx-80, discharge rate above 20C, the raw CFx discharge rate performance deteriorated and showed a poor rate performance. The maximum discharge rate of CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are 80C, indicating that the modified CFx rate performance is improved. CFx-80 possesses the largest specific capacity and specific energy increase rate relative to raw CFx at 50C, which are 142.6 % and 88.4 %, respectively. The power density of Li/CFx battery is related to the discharge time and energy density. Power density and discharge time are shown in Table S4. At the discharge rate of 80C, the power density of CFx-30, CFx-50 and CFx-80 are 129.552, 134.091 and 118.180 kW/kg, respectively. With the increase of microwave modification power, the corresponding discharge time of Li/CFx battery increases at same discharge rate, which indicates that microwave modification is an effective way to boost rate performance of CFx.

Compared with previously reported by literatures [15,26,31,48–52] in Fig. 6
(a), the corresponding data was listed in Table S5. The Li/CFx battery in this study shows unprecedented rate capabilities, power density and energy density. To further study the effect of CFx with –OH groups and raw CFx on electron density and band gap, the CASTEP was utilized to calculate the density of states (DOS) for various CFx structures based on the FT-IR results. According to the optimized crystal structure models (consisting of 32 fluorine atoms and 32 carbon atoms) in Fig. 6 (b), single and randomly distributed –OH groups were introduced to raw CFx. The models of optimized crystal structure and corresponding Fermi energy and DOS are shown in Fig. 6 (b). The raw CFx exhibits the electrochemical property of a semiconductor, as evidenced by the position of its Fermi energy at the boundary of the band gap. –OH groups act as electron donors grafted on raw CFx, resulting in a change in the electronic distribution on CFx surface. Meanwhile, the band gap of these modified CFx models become narrower. The band gap of CFx with randomly distributed –OH groups, CFx with single –OH groups and raw CFx are 2.833, 2.904 and 3.142 eV, respectively. The introduction of –OH on CFx surface improves the wettability between CFx surface and the electrolyte, which reduces interface resistance beneficial for rapid ion migration. Therefore, grafting –OH groups on CFx increase the energy density and power density in Li/CFx battery. The electron density combined with potential of the raw CFx and modified CFx are shown in Fig. S8. Local –OH grafting changes the electron density and disrupts the balance of CFx, forming a local high potential region, which is more conducive to the chemical reaction between CFx and low potential lithium metal, thereby improving electrochemical performance. These characteristics suggest that modified CFx exhibits the electrochemical characteristics of a semiconductor. The alteration in DOS and electron density suggests that the incorporation of –OH groups leads to electron redistribution. Consequently, the CFx containing –OH groups exhibits enhanced electrochemical characteristics.

A facial and effective alcohol-based microwave-induction method was developed to modify the surface of CFx to boost high-rate performance of Li/CFx battery. Appling microwave-induction strategy, C-F bond regulation, functional group grafting and CQDs construction on the surface of CFx reduce the resistance of CFx and increase the diffusion coefficient of lithium ion. Meanwhile, the modified CFx achieves defluorination, reduces the fluorine to carbon ratio and discharge polarization of CFx. Compared with raw CFx, the microwave-modified CFx achieves 80C high discharge rate as well as high power density of 134.091 kW/kg. Interestingly, the modified CFx possesses the largest specific capacity increase ratio of 142.6 % at 50C.
The financial support for this work was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51972045).

Gaobang Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yong Fan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tianhui Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Muhammad Shoaib: Investigation, Formal analysis. Azim Khan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yifan Liu: Supervision, Project administration. Xian Jian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support for the aforementioned funding and the contributions of each author. This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51972045). Besides, the authors express their gratitude to all the reviewers who took part in the review process.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.154899.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
